content.text:
we are in a big berlin hackathon and we want to go for peec ai track
their challange:
- Challenge
    
    AI is compressing product, hiring, and even fundraising advantages - distribution becomes the moat. Peec AI tracks how brands appear across LLMs - how visible it is, how it's talked about and how you compare to competitors.
    
    Participants must use the Peec AI MCP to build something that helps an early-stage brand win distribution against bigger competitors. That can mean AI agents, content machines, lifecycle, launch workflows - or anything else that helps small, early-stage teams close the gap with much bigger teams. Creativity matters.
    
    All teams will have access to Peec AI test projects built around the brands below.
    
    **Example brands**
    
    *Nothing Phone vs Apple & Samsung*
    Design‑first, transparent, AI‑native phones taking on the default choices in smartphones. Build something that helps Nothing dominate attention, trust, and consideration across the channels that matter most.
    
    *Attio vs Salesforce & HubSpot*
    A modern, flexible CRM for teams that hate traditional CRMs, going up against heavyweight stacks like Salesforce and HubSpot. Build something that helps Attio win more of the market’s mindshare and customer journey, from discovery to conversion to retention.
    
    *BYD vs Tesla & Legacy Automakers*
    The world’s top EV seller in units, still treated like the outsider brand in many markets. Build something that helps BYD own more of the conversation around EVs in the channels where buyers are making decisions.
    
    Docs: https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/introduction




I want you to use also this documentaion website: https://docs.peec.ai/intro-to-peec-ai to give me the tools I can use the MCP and so on.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- gpt-image-2-prompting: Build and tune prompts for OpenAI's gpt-image-2 (and gpt-image-1/1.5/1-mini) — covers text-to-image generation and image edits including style transfer, inpainting, compositing, virtual try-on, identity-preserving edits, logos, ads, UI mockups, infographics, slides, scientific diagrams, product mockups, lighting/weather transforms, sketch-to-render, object removal, character consistency, and multi-image referencing. Also covers choosing between the Image API (`images.generate`/`images.edit`) and the Responses API image_generation tool (multi-turn editing, `previous_response_id`, revised prompts), mask-based inpainting, streaming with `partial_images`, size/quality/format/background/compression parameters, moderation, org verification, and cost/latency tradeoffs. Use whenever the user is writing or refining prompts for `client.images.generate`, `client.images.edit`, or the Responses API `image_generation` tool; deciding model/quality/size/format parameters; debugging image output quality; or constructing prompts sent to `/v1/images/generations` or `/v1/images/edits`. Sources: OpenAI Cookbook "GPT Image Generation Models Prompting Guide" + OpenAI Image Generation API docs.
- interfaces-that-feel: Apply emotional intelligence and craft to any user-facing interface. Use when building UI components, pages, flows, or writing copy — especially when the result feels technically correct but flat, generic, or soulless. Also use when asked to "review my UI", "improve this design", "make this feel better", "audit UX", or "add polish". Evaluates and builds through the feel lens: voice, earned emotion, and restraint. Covers motion craft, copy voice, empty states, error messages, loading states, onboarding, and micro-interactions. Reference aesthetic: How We Feel, Headspace, Gentler Streak, Amie, Arc Browser — warm, considered, emotionally intelligent.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Include any feature names and how they're structured.

content.content:
# Peec AI Overview

## Core Purpose
Peec AI is an AI search analytics platform that tracks how brands appear across AI platforms like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Copilot, providing visibility data that differs fundamentally from traditional search rankings.

## Primary Metrics Tracked

**Visibility**: Frequency of brand mentions in AI responses across platforms, functioning as a market share indicator for AI conversations.

**Position**: Ranking placement when a brand appears in AI responses relative to competitors.

**Sentiment**: Whether AI models describe brands positively, neutrally, or negatively when mentioning them.

## Core Concepts

- **Sources**: Websites and articles that AI models reference when mentioning brands—the foundation driving all metrics
- **Prompts**: Conversational questions (e.g., "best CRM for marketing agencies under 50 people?") rather than keywords, reflecting authentic user behavior
- **Projects**: Organizational structures for tracking specific industries or initiatives
- **Models**: AI platforms being monitored (ChatGPT, Gemini, Copilot, etc.)

## Key Capabilities

The platform enables users to:
- Monitor visibility trends over time
- Identify competitor positioning
- Discover which sources influence AI responses
- Spot content gaps where brands are cited but not mentioned
- Access data through exports, Looker connectors, and APIs (Enterprise)

## Distinguishing Technology

Peec AI employs "advanced UI scraping technology" to simulate authentic user interactions rather than relying on APIs, ensuring data reflects real user experiences rather than sanitized API responses.

content.content:
# Peec AI MCP Server - Tools & Setup Summary

## Installation & Connection

**Server URL:** `https://api.peec.ai/mcp`

**Transport:** Streamable HTTP

**Authentication:** OAuth 2.0 with browser-based consent flow

The MCP integrates with Claude, Cursor, VS Code (GitHub Copilot), and Windsurf. Users authorize once, and the session persists across conversations.

## Available Tools

The documentation references tools but **does not provide a detailed list** of individual tool names, parameters, or return types. However, it indicates two categories:

### Read-Only Tools (Data Access)
- Brand visibility metrics across 8 AI engines (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, Google AI Overviews, Google AI Mode, Claude, Microsoft Copilot, Grok)
- Competitive analysis (visibility, sentiment, share of voice, position)
- Source analysis and cited URL inspection
- Scraped markdown retrieval of cited pages
- Trend analysis by date, model, topic, or country
- "Ranked next steps from Peec Actions: opportunity-scored recommendations grouped by owned pages, editorial coverage, reference sites, and UGC communities"

### Write Tools (Configuration Management)
- Create, edit, delete prompts, topics, tags, and tracked brands
- All write operations require confirmation before execution
- Delete operations flagged as `destructiveHint: true`
- Require organization-owner access

## Built-In Workflows

Pre-configured slash command prompts include:
- Weekly visibility pulse
- Engine scorecard
- Topic heatmap
- Campaign tracker

## Requirements

- Active Peec AI account with ≥1 project
- Supported AI tool/platform

For complete tool specifications, the documentation directs users to `https://docs.peec.ai/llms.txt` and a dedicated [tools reference page](/mcp/tools).

content.content:
# Peec AI Documentation Index

## MCP Server & Tools
- **MCP Server**: "Connect AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, and other MCP-compatible tools directly to your Peec AI data"
  - https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/introduction.md
- **MCP Setup Guide**: Step-by-step connection instructions for Claude and Cursor
  - https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/setup.md
- **MCP Tools Reference**: Complete parameter and response field documentation
  - https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/tools.md
- **MCP Use Cases**: Example workflows and analysis scenarios
  - https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/use-cases.md
- **MCP Prompts**: "Peec AI ships native MCP prompts: ready-to-run analysis workflows exposed as slash commands"
  - https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/prompts.md

## Core API Endpoints
**Project Management**: List Projects, Create/Update/Delete Brands, Prompts, Topics, Tags
**Suggestions**: Accept/Reject Brand, Prompt, and Topic suggestions
**Data Access**: List Chats, Fanout queries, Model Channels
**Reports**: Brands, Domains, URLs, URL Content
- Full reference: https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/

## Key Concepts
- Metrics: Position, Sentiment, Share of Voice, Visibility
- Understanding: Chats, Sources, Performance, Crawlability
- Setup: Prompts, Organization, Project Management
- Reporting: Looker Studio connector available

**Authentication & Limits**: https://docs.peec.ai/api/authentication.md and https://docs.peec.ai/api/ratelimits.md

content.content:
# Peec AI MCP Tools Reference

## Read-Only Tools (15)

1. **list_projects**
   - Description: Lists all accessible projects
   - Parameters: None
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `name`, `status`

2. **list_brands**
   - Description: "List brands (your brand and tracked competitors) in a project"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `limit` (number), `offset` (number)
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `name`, `domains`, `aliases`, `is_own`

3. **list_topics**
   - Description: Lists topic groupings where prompts are organized
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `limit` (number), `offset` (number)
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `name`

4. **list_tags**
   - Description: "Lists tags (cross-cutting labels) in a project"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `limit` (number), `offset` (number)
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `name`

5. **list_models**
   - Description: "Lists all AI engines (models) configured for a project"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required)
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `name`, `is_active`

6. **list_prompts**
   - Description: Lists prompts with optional topic or tag filtering
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `topic_id`, `tag_id`, `limit`, `offset`
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `text`, `tag_ids`, `topic_id`, `volume`

7. **list_chats**
   - Description: "List individual AI responses (chats) for a project over a date range"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (all required); `brand_id`, `prompt_id`, `model_id`, `limit`, `offset`
   - Returns: Columns with `id`, `prompt_id`, `model_id`, `date`

8. **get_chat**
   - Description: "Get the full content of a single chat"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `chat_id` (string, required)
   - Returns: Direct object with `messages`, `brands_mentioned`, `sources`, `queries`, `products`, `prompt`, `model`

9. **list_search_queries**
   - Description: "Lists the search sub-queries an AI engine fanned out to while answering prompts"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (required); `prompt_id`, `chat_id`, `model_id`, `model_channel_id`, `topic_id`, `tag_id`, `limit`, `offset`
   - Returns: Columns with `prompt_id`, `chat_id`, `model_id`, `model_channel_id`, `date`, `query_index`, `query_text`

10. **list_shopping_queries**
    - Description: Lists product/shopping sub-queries issued during AI responses
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (required); `prompt_id`, `chat_id`, `model_id`, `model_channel_id`, `topic_id`, `tag_id`, `limit`, `offset`
    - Returns: Columns with `prompt_id`, `chat_id`, `model_id`, `model_channel_id`, `date`, `query_text`, `products`

11. **get_brand_report**
    - Description: "Returns brand visibility, sentiment, position, and share of voice across AI search engines"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (required); `limit`, `offset`, `dimensions`, `filters`
    - Returns: Columns with `brand_id`, `brand_name`, `visibility`, `mention_count`, `share_of_voice`, `sentiment`, `position`

12. **get_domain_report**
    - Description: "Returns source domain retrieval and citation metrics across AI search engines"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (required); `limit`, `offset`, `dimensions`, `filters`
    - Returns: Columns with `domain`, `classification`, `retrieved_percentage`, `retrieval_rate`, `citation_rate`, `mentioned_brand_ids`

13. **get_url_report**
    - Description: "Returns URL-level retrieval and citation metrics across AI search engines"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date` (required); `limit`, `offset`, `dimensions`, `filters`
    - Returns: Columns with `url`, `classification`, `title`, `channel_title`, `citation_count`, `retrievals`, `citation_rate`, `mentioned_brand_ids`

14. **get_url_content**
    - Description: "Returns the scraped markdown content of a source URL Peec has indexed"
    - Parameters: `project_id` (string, required), `url` (string, required), `max_length` (number)
    - Returns: Object with `url`, `title`, `domain`, `channel_title`, `classification`, `url_classification`, `content`, `content_length`, `truncated`, `content_updated_at`

15. **get_actions**
    - Description: "Returns Peec's opportunity-scored action recommendations for a project"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date`, `scope` (all required); `tag_ids`, `topic_ids`, `model_ids`, `model_channel_ids`, `country_codes`, `url_classification`, `domain`
    - Returns: For `overview` scope: `action_group_type`, `url_classification`, `domain`, `opportunity_score`, `relative_opportunity_score`, support metrics. For other scopes: `text`, `group_type`, `url_classification`, `domain`, scores

---

## Write Tools (12)

1. **create_brand**
   - Description: "Create a new tracked brand in a project (own brand or competitor)"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `domains`, `aliases`, `regex`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

2. **update_brand**
   - Description: "Update a brand's name, regex, aliases, or domains"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `brand_id` (both required), `name`, `domains`, `aliases`, `regex`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

3. **delete_brand**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a brand" [destructive]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `brand_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

4. **create_prompt**
   - Description: "Create a new prompt in a project. May consume plan credits"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `text`, `country_code` (all required), `topic_id`, `tag_ids`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

5. **update_prompt**
   - Description: "Update a prompt's topic and/or tags"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `prompt_id` (both required), `topic_id`, `tag_ids`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

6. **delete_prompt**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a prompt" [destructive, cascades to chats]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `prompt_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

7. **create_tag**
   - Description: "Create a new tag for cross-cutting prompt labels"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `color`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

8. **update_tag**
   - Description: "Update a tag's name or color"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `tag_id` (both required), `name`, `color`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

9. **delete_tag**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a tag and detach from all prompts" [destructive]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `tag_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

10. **create_topic**
    - Description: "Create a new topic to group related prompts"
    - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `country_code`
    - Returns: Object with `id`

11. **update_topic**
    - Description: "Rename a topic"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `topic_id` (both required), `name`
    - Returns: Object with `success: true`

12. **delete_topic**
    - Description: "Soft-delete a topic" [destructive, detaches prompts]
    - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `topic_id` (required)
    - Returns: Object with `success: true`

    - Returns: Object with `url`, `title`, `domain`, `channel_title`, `classification`, `url_classification`, `content`, `content_length`, `truncated`, `content_updated_at`

15. **get_actions**
    - Description: "Returns Peec's opportunity-scored action recommendations for a project"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `start_date`, `end_date`, `scope` (all required); `tag_ids`, `topic_ids`, `model_ids`, `model_channel_ids`, `country_codes`, `url_classification`, `domain`
    - Returns: For `overview` scope: `action_group_type`, `url_classification`, `domain`, `opportunity_score`, `relative_opportunity_score`, support metrics. For other scopes: `text`, `group_type`, `url_classification`, `domain`, scores

---

## Write Tools (12)

1. **create_brand**
   - Description: "Create a new tracked brand in a project (own brand or competitor)"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `domains`, `aliases`, `regex`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

2. **update_brand**
   - Description: "Update a brand's name, regex, aliases, or domains"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `brand_id` (both required), `name`, `domains`, `aliases`, `regex`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

3. **delete_brand**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a brand" [destructive]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `brand_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

4. **create_prompt**
   - Description: "Create a new prompt in a project. May consume plan credits"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `text`, `country_code` (all required), `topic_id`, `tag_ids`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

5. **update_prompt**
   - Description: "Update a prompt's topic and/or tags"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `prompt_id` (both required), `topic_id`, `tag_ids`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

6. **delete_prompt**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a prompt" [destructive, cascades to chats]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `prompt_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

7. **create_tag**
   - Description: "Create a new tag for cross-cutting prompt labels"
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `color`
   - Returns: Object with `id`

8. **update_tag**
   - Description: "Update a tag's name or color"
   - Parameters: `project_id`, `tag_id` (both required), `name`, `color`
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

9. **delete_tag**
   - Description: "Soft-delete a tag and detach from all prompts" [destructive]
   - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `tag_id` (required)
   - Returns: Object with `success: true`

10. **create_topic**
    - Description: "Create a new topic to group related prompts"
    - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `name` (required), `country_code`
    - Returns: Object with `id`

11. **update_topic**
    - Description: "Rename a topic"
    - Parameters: `project_id`, `topic_id` (both required), `name`
    - Returns: Object with `success: true`

12. **delete_topic**
    - Description: "Soft-delete a topic" [destructive, detaches prompts]
    - Parameters: `project_id` (required), `topic_id` (required)
    - Returns: Object with `success: true`

input.prompt: List every native MCP prompt / slash command Peec AI ships. For each: name, what it does, what arguments it takes, and what output it produces. Be exhaustive.

List every example workflow or use case mentioned for Peec AI MCP. For each: name, the question/goal it answers, and the sequence of MCP tools used. Include any ideas around content workflows, competitor monitoring, lifecycle automation, launches, or AI agents.

content.content:
# Peec AI Native MCP Prompts

## 1. **peec_weekly_pulse**
- **Function**: "Week-over-week digest covering brand metrics, competitor movers, source shifts, and sentiment flags"
- **Arguments**: `project` (required; name or ID)
- **Output**: Formatted internal-sharing snapshot of weekly changes
- **Use case**: Quick Monday assessments of what shifted week-to-week

## 2. **peec_competitor_radar**
- **Function**: Scans brand × topic × engine combinations for significant visibility shifts, identifying major gainers/losers and exposure areas
- **Arguments**: `project` (required); `threshold_pp` (optional, default 10 percentage points)
- **Output**: Biggest movers, hypothesized drivers, competitive exposure zones
- **Use case**: Understanding sudden competitor visibility changes and their sources

## 3. **peec_engine_scorecard**
- **Function**: "Per-engine breakdown of brand visibility, share of voice, sentiment, position" plus source retrieval and citation metrics
- **Arguments**: `project` (required)
- **Output**: Engine-by-engine performance report highlighting untapped potential
- **Use case**: Prioritizing which AI engine deserves focus next

## 4. **peec_topic_heatmap**
- **Function**: "Visibility heatmap across all your topics and all tracked AI engines" calibrated to baseline performance levels
- **Arguments**: `project` (required)
- **Output**: Grid labeled with blind spots, weak/moderate/strong/dominant cells
- **Use case**: Identifying visibility gaps and strongholds across dimensions

## 5. **peec_prompt_grader**
- **Function**: "Grades your tracked prompt set on topic balance, tag hygiene, funnel coverage, branded ratio, duplicate detection, and model data gaps"
- **Arguments**: `project` (required)
- **Output**: A-F report card with top 3 recommended fixes
- **Use case**: Auditing prompt set quality and completeness

## 6. **peec_source_authority**
- **Function**: Audits domain performance as AI sources, measuring retrieval/citation rates, top URLs, content formats, and authority gaps
- **Arguments**: `project` (required)
- **Output**: Authority performance breakdown identifying citation opportunities vs. competitors
- **Use case**: Optimizing content for AI engine pickup and citation

## 7. **peec_campaign_tracker**
- **Function**: "Before/after comparison around a launch date" tracking brand metrics and source pickup by engine
- **Arguments**: `project` (required); `campaign_date` (required, YYYY-MM-DD format); `urls` (optional, comma-separated)
- **Output**: Campaign impact analysis engine-by-engine
- **Use case**: Measuring PR, guest posts, or content refreshes' AI visibility impact

content.content:
# Peec AI MCP Workflows & Use Cases

## Core Analytics Workflows

**1. Brand Visibility Overview**
- Goal: "How visible is my brand across AI search engines this month?"
- Tools: Brand visibility retrieval, sentiment scoring, share of voice calculation

**2. Competitive Benchmarking by AI Model**
- Goal: Compare visibility across ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and other platforms
- Tools: Multi-brand report generation, model-level filtering

**3. Source Citation Analysis**
- Goal: "Which of our pages get cited most by AI engines?"
- Tools: `get_url_report`, retrieval counting, page ranking

**4. Topic Deep-Dive**
- Goal: Brand performance comparison within specific topics
- Tools: Topic-filtered visibility, sentiment, share of voice metrics

**5. Visibility Trend Analysis**
- Goal: "Show me our visibility trend over the last 30 days"
- Tools: Time-series data with change highlighting

## Competitive Analysis Workflows

**6. Share of Voice Comparison**
- Goal: Mention share versus competitive set
- Tools: Brand reporting with temporal filtering

**7. Sentiment Comparison**
- Goal: Positivity comparison across specific competitors
- Tools: Sentiment-filtered brand reports by model

**8. Position Analysis**
- Goal: Identify consistent top-position mentions across platforms
- Tools: Position ranking aggregation

## Source & Citation Workflows

**9. Top Cited Domains**
- Goal: Understand authority distribution in your space
- Tools: Domain-level citation ranking

**10. Your Domain Performance**
- Goal: Retrieval and citation frequency by AI model
- Tools: `get_url_report` dimensioned by model

**11. Competitor Source Analysis**
- Goal: Identify authoritative competitor domains
- Tools: Citation rate comparison across domains

**12. Content Gap via URL Inspection**
- Goal: Compare competitor URL structure and depth to your own
- Tools: `get_url_content` for indexed markdown, content comparison

## Reporting & Insights Workflows

**13. Weekly Performance Summary**
- Goal: "Give me a weekly summary: our visibility, share of voice, and sentiment for the past 7 days vs the previous 7 days."
- Tools: Time-window comparison metrics

**14. Geographic Breakdown**
- Goal: Country-level visibility variation
- Tools: Regional filtering on brand reports

**15. Content Gap Analysis**
- Goal: "Which topics have high competitor visibility but low visibility for our brand?"
- Tools: Topic-level competitive gap identification

## Strategic Planning Workflows

**16. Ranked Quarterly Opportunities**
- Goal: "What should we focus on next quarter to improve AI visibility?"
- Tools: Opportunity scoring across owned pages, editorial, reference sites, UGC

**17. Opportunity Drill-Down**
- Goal: Concrete actions for high-priority opportunities (e.g., YouTube UGC)
- Tools: Domain-specific outreach recommendations, creator suggestions

## Project Management Workflows

**18. Add New Tracked Prompt**
- Goal: Expand tracking scope with new search queries
- Tools: `create_prompt`, tag resolution, project linking

**19. Reorganize Prompts by Topic**
- Goal: Create topic groups and bulk-move related prompts
- Tools: `list_prompts`, `create_topic`, `update_prompt`

**20. Track New Competitor**
- Goal: "Start tracking Acme as a competitor. Their domains are..."
- Tools: `create_brand` with domain and alias registration

**21. Clean Up Stale Tags**
- Goal: Remove low-usage organizational tags
- Tools: `list_tags`, `list_prompts`, `delete_tag` with confirmation

## Advanced Workflows (MCP Challenge)

**22. Fix Outdated Information in AI Sources**
- Goal: Locate and outreach for pricing/feature corrections across cited URLs
- Tools: `get_url_report`, `get_domain_report`, `get_url_content`, outreach drafting

**23. Identify Declining Content Citations**
- Goal: "For our domain, show URLs whose retrieval rate dropped most over 90 days"
- Tools: `get_url_report` with time-window delta comparison

**24. Prompt-Level Competitive Gap**
- Goal: "For every prompt we track, rank where a competitor beats us most"
- Tools: `get_brand_report` dimensioned by `prompt_id`, `list_prompts`

**25. Negative Sentiment Source Identification**
- Goal: "Which URLs are AI citing when describing us negatively?"
- Tools: `get_url_report`, `list_chats`, `get_chat`, sentiment cross-reference

content.text:
Here's the full Peec AI MCP toolkit pulled from the docs, plus angles that fit the "small brand vs giants" framing.

## Connection

- **Server**: `https://api.peec.ai/mcp` (Streamable HTTP, OAuth 2.0)
- **Clients**: Claude, Cursor, VS Code Copilot, Windsurf
- **Requires**: Active Peec account + ≥1 project (you'll get the test projects: Nothing, Attio, BYD)
- **Setup steps**: https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/setup

## Read-only tools (15) — the data layer

| Tool | What it gives you |
|---|---|
| `list_projects` | All accessible projects |
| `list_brands` | Your brand + tracked competitors (id, domains, aliases, is_own) |
| `list_topics` / `list_tags` | Topic/tag taxonomy in a project |
| `list_models` | Which engines are tracked (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, Google AIO, AI Mode, Claude, Copilot, Grok) |
| `list_prompts` | Prompts being tracked (with volume) |
| `list_chats` | Individual AI responses by date/brand/prompt/model |
| `get_chat` | Full message + brands_mentioned + sources + queries + products for one chat |
| `list_search_queries` | The sub-queries an engine fanned out to |
| `list_shopping_queries` | Product/shopping sub-queries |
| `get_brand_report` | **visibility, mention_count, share_of_voice, sentiment, position** per brand (with dimensions/filters) |
| `get_domain_report` | Per-domain retrieval %, retrieval_rate, citation_rate |
| `get_url_report` | Per-URL retrieval, citations, classification, mentioned brands |
| `get_url_content` | Scraped markdown of any cited page |
| `get_actions` | **Opportunity-scored next steps** grouped by owned pages / editorial / reference sites / UGC |

## Write tools (12) — configuration

`create_brand` / `update_brand` / `delete_brand`, `create_prompt` / `update_prompt` / `delete_prompt`, `create_tag` / `update_tag` / `delete_tag`, `create_topic` / `update_topic` / `delete_topic`. All writes confirm before executing; deletes are flagged destructive.

## Native slash-command prompts (built-in workflows)

- `peec_weekly_pulse` — week-over-week digest
- `peec_competitor_radar` — biggest movers + drivers (`threshold_pp` default 10)
- `peec_engine_scorecard` — per-engine breakdown of where you're under-indexed
- `peec_topic_heatmap` — visibility grid: blind spots vs strongholds
- `peec_prompt_grader` — A–F audit of your tracked prompt set + top 3 fixes
- `peec_source_authority` — domain/URL authority audit vs competitors
- `peec_campaign_tracker` — before/after a `campaign_date` + optional `urls`

These are huge — most of them are probably 80% of what a naive hackathon submission would do. Build **on top of** them.

## Hackathon angles that fit the "distribution moat" challenge

The judges said: AI agents, content machines, lifecycle, launch workflows. Underdogs win when they (a) find the few prompts where they can actually beat giants, (b) move faster on the cited sources, and (c) close the loop to action.

**1. "Wedge Finder" agent — pick winnable battles**
For Nothing/Attio/BYD, most prompts are dominated by Apple/HubSpot/Tesla. Use `get_brand_report` dimensioned by `prompt_id` × `model_id` to find the 5–10 prompts with the smallest gap and decent volume. Then `get_url_report` on those prompts → identify the *specific* cited URLs to target. Output: a ranked battle plan, not a dashboard.
*Tools: list_prompts → get_brand_report → get_url_report → get_actions*

**2. Cited-source content machine**
For each winnable prompt, `get_url_content` on the top 3 cited URLs from competitors → feed to Claude → generate a better-structured page draft that hits the same sub-queries (`list_search_queries`) the engine actually fans out to. Ship as Markdown + a Notion/HubSpot draft.
*Differentiator: most teams will dump rankings; you'll dump publishable artifacts.*

**3. Lifecycle/launch radar with auto-response**
Wrap `peec_campaign_tracker`: when a launch URL fails to get picked up by engine X within N days, auto-trigger outreach drafts (Gmail MCP is available) to the top reference domains for that topic. Closes the loop campaigns → measurement → next action.

**4. Sentiment firefighter**
`list_chats` filtered to your brand + negative sentiment → `get_chat` → extract `sources` → `get_url_content` → draft correction/outreach email per source. Daily Slack digest of "URLs damaging you right now."

**5. UGC/Reddit swarm planner**
`get_actions` with `scope=overview` returns opportunity scores grouped by **UGC communities**. Build an agent that, for the top UGC group per topic, drafts authentic-sounding seed posts/comments + identifies subreddits/threads. (Be careful with the framing — present as "community engagement briefs for the team" not "astroturf.")

**6. Prompt-set auto-evolver**
`peec_prompt_grader` finds gaps → agent generates new prompts to balance funnel/topic coverage → `create_prompt` (with confirmation). Plus weekly check: if a prompt has zero volume after 30 days, suggest deletion. Keeps the moat self-maintaining.

## My recommendation

For a 1–2 day build, **#1 + #2 combined** is the sharpest pitch: "An agent that finds the 5 prompts a small brand can realistically win this quarter, then ships the content drafts and outreach to win them." It's concrete, demo-able in 90 seconds, and directly answers "close the gap with bigger teams." The Peec MCP gives you the targeting; Claude gives you the artifacts.

Want me to scaffold the agent (e.g., a small TypeScript or Python project that wires the Peec MCP + Claude API)? If yes, tell me which brand you're picking and which client (Claude Code, Cursor, custom).
I really liked 5

User is at a Berlin hackathon working on the Peec AI track. Challenge: help an early-stage brand win distribution against bigger competitors using the Peec AI MCP (https://api.peec.ai/mcp). Test brands available: Nothing (vs Apple/Samsung), Attio (vs Salesforce/HubSpot), BYD (vs Tesla/legacy automakers).

Direction chosen: **UGC/community advocacy planner** (idea #5 from initial brainstorm). Uses Peec's `get_actions` with `scope=overview` to find UGC opportunity groups, then produces community engagement artifacts (subreddit/thread targets, talking-point briefs for fans, response templates).

**Why:** Small brands can't outspend giants on paid distribution but they have something giants don't — passionate early communities (Nothing fans, EV enthusiasts, anti-Salesforce CRM crowd). The Peec MCP can quantify which UGC channels actually feed AI engines.

**How to apply:** Frame all output as enabling authentic advocates / community managers, NOT as astroturf generation. Judges will care about the ethics framing. Differentiator vs other hackathon teams: ship publishable artifacts, not dashboards.

content.text:
Good pick. #5 is actually the strongest angle for this challenge because it attacks the one asymmetric advantage small brands have — **passionate early communities that giants can't manufacture**. Apple doesn't have fans who'd write a 2000-word Reddit post defending the Vision Pro the way Nothing fans defend Carl Pei. Let me sharpen it before we build.

## Reframing (this matters for judging)

Don't pitch this as "Reddit swarm" — that reads as astroturf and judges will mark you down. Pitch it as:

> **"Advocate Enablement Engine"** — an agent that finds the exact community threads where AI engines are *already* listening, then equips a brand's existing fans/community managers with researched briefs to participate authentically.

Same product. Defensible framing. The Peec data justifies it: `get_url_report` shows Reddit/YouTube/forum URLs that AI engines actually cite. You're not seeding noise — you're showing where the signal already flows.

## Concrete architecture

**Input:** Project ID (e.g., Nothing) + optional topic filter

**Pipeline:**
1. `get_actions` with `scope=overview` → get UGC opportunity groups ranked by `opportunity_score`
2. For top N UGC groups: `get_actions` with `scope=ugc_communities` (or whatever the drilled scope is) → specific recommendations
3. `get_url_report` filtered to UGC `url_classification` → which Reddit threads / YouTube videos / forum posts AI engines are *currently* citing for competitor topics
4. `get_url_content` on top 5 → understand what the cited content actually says
5. `list_chats` + `get_chat` for prompts in those topics → see which UGC URLs show up in real AI responses and what brands those responses recommend
6. **Claude generates per-thread "advocate brief"**: thread URL, why it matters (cited by ChatGPT for X prompt), 3 honest talking points the brand's strengths support, what NOT to say, links to back up claims

**Output (the demo artifact):**
A "Community Action Pack" PDF/Notion page per week — 10 prioritized threads, with for each: the AI prompt it influences, current sentiment, an honest brief a real fan or community manager could use.

## What makes this demo well in 90 seconds

- Open Claude/Cursor → run `/advocate_pack project=nothing`
- Live shows: "Found 47 Reddit threads cited by ChatGPT for 'best Android phone for design 2026'. Apple wins 8/10. Here are 3 threads where Nothing has a realistic opening — drafting briefs now..."
- Output: actual markdown briefs the judges can read

The "live data → artifact" loop is what beats dashboard demos.

Two things I need from you before I scaffold code:

questions.question: Which test brand do you want to build the demo around?

options.label: Nothing Phone

content.text: [Request interrupted by user for tool use]

give us something niche to works on, what would be the best use case, we really want to work on UGC content, like give us help to create UGC contents by the user and we actually show in the hackathon it is working through peec ai. Be super critisize about the idea.

Cool, let me be brutal.

## The killer flaw with "UGC + show it working in Peec"

There's a latency problem you have to confront before you build anything: **AI engines don't index your UGC instantly.** A Reddit post takes days to weeks to show up in ChatGPT's retrieval. Perplexity is fastest (live web search, hours). Gemini and ChatGPT browse mode are next. ChatGPT/Claude without browse may take weeks or never.

If your demo is "we generate a Reddit post → Peec shows it being cited," you will fail live. The judges will ask "show me the data" and you'll have nothing. **This is the #1 reason to not pick this idea naively.**

You have to design the demo around this constraint, not pretend it doesn't exist.

## The niche I'd actually pick

**"Stack Overflow & GitHub answer engine for Attio (or any dev-tool brand)"** — and I'll defend why this is the only UGC angle that actually works in 48 hours.

Why this niche, brutally:
- **Stack Overflow + GitHub get re-crawled by Perplexity within hours.** Both are in the absolute top tier of cited sources for technical AI prompts. Verifiable.
- **Attio's audience IS developers and founders.** Generating a SO answer in their voice is plausible; generating a Reddit post pretending to be a Nothing fan is not.
- **The content is naturally factual.** "How do I migrate from HubSpot to Attio's API?" → real code, real answer. No astroturf vibe. Judges can't object.
- **Comparison-intent prompts ("best X for Y") get fanned out to specific sub-queries** that you can target precisely with a single SO answer or a GitHub README.
- Most hackathon teams will pick Nothing because it's flashier. Going Attio + dev-niche is contrarian and the demo has actual proof.

## What you actually build

**"Citation Bait" — a Peec-driven UGC brief generator + auto-publisher for technical channels.**

Pipeline:
1. `list_prompts` filtered to comparison/migration intent ("Attio vs HubSpot", "best CRM API")
2. `list_search_queries` to get the *fanout* sub-queries the engines actually run — this is your real targeting layer, nobody else uses this
3. `get_url_report` → which Stack Overflow / GitHub / dev.to URLs get cited for those sub-queries
4. `get_url_content` on top 5 → understand the answer pattern that wins citations
5. Claude generates a *better* answer/README/gist that hits the same sub-queries with Attio context
6. **Publish to a real account you control** (a personal SO/GitHub account you make for the demo)
7. Re-query Perplexity 2-4 hours later, show the citation

## Why be skeptical of even this idea

Things that can sink it on demo day:
- **Perplexity might not pick it up in your timeframe.** Mitigation: publish *immediately* when you arrive at the venue, not 2 hours before demo. Buy 24+ hours of indexing time.
- **Stack Overflow may flag your account as low-rep and rate-limit it.** Mitigation: use a real, aged account (yours). Don't make a fresh one for the demo.
- **The answer must be genuinely good or it gets downvoted, which signals "spam" to crawlers.** Mitigation: this is why you target *technical* questions where Claude can write a legitimately useful answer with code.
- **One citation is not a product.** Mitigation: also show the *replay* — "here's the brief our tool would have written for [existing high-citation URL] based purely on Peec data, compared to what was actually published." This proves the system works even if your live test doesn't index in time.
- **It's still a content generator under the hood.** Your moat must be visibly the prompt-fanout targeting. Make the demo screen literally show: "ChatGPT fans out 'Attio vs HubSpot' to these 7 sub-queries → 6 of them have a winning citation pattern → here's the gap."

## What you put on the demo screen

Side-by-side, three panels:
1. **Left:** Peec MCP calls firing live — fanout queries, cited URLs, gaps
2. **Center:** The generated brief + actual published Stack Overflow/GitHub link (clickable, judges can verify)
3. **Right:** Perplexity query window — re-run the original prompt, show the new citation appearing (if it indexed) OR show a replay of an equivalent past piece that did get cited

If Perplexity doesn't cite you in time, the right panel becomes the replay. You always have a story.

## The single biggest risk I haven't addressed

Spamming Stack Overflow / GitHub during a hackathon to win a competition is **borderline policy-violating** on those platforms even if the content is good. Be ready for a judge to ask "isn't this just SEO spam?" Your answer has to be: *"The tool generates briefs the brand's existing developer advocates write themselves — we're demoing the loop with one author (me) for proof, but the product is brief-generation for real community managers."* If you can't say that with a straight face, pick a different idea.

---

Want me to scaffold this as a Python/TS CLI that wires the Peec MCP, runs the pipeline, and outputs a brief + a Perplexity verification query? Tell me language preference and whether you want to auto-publish to GitHub (I can wire the GitHub API safely) or keep publishing manual.
look at it from different prespectives, come up with new idea for the hackathon

OK. Let me throw out the UGC frame entirely and look at this from angles I bet other teams won't.

## Perspective 1: "Stop marketing, start listening"

**The shift:** Everyone at this hackathon will build *outbound* tools (publish, outreach, SEO). Flip it: Peec data is the richest source of customer research nobody's mining. AI engines fan out users' real questions and reproduce competitor pain points. That's a market research goldmine.

**What you build:** **"Voice of the Market"** — mines `list_chats` + `get_chat` across competitor-mentioning prompts to extract: unmet feature requests, recurring complaints about Salesforce/Apple/Tesla, jobs-to-be-done patterns. Output: a weekly product brief for the underdog's PM team. ("AI-described HubSpot pain points this week: 1. Pricing opacity (mentioned in 47 chats), 2. Workflow rigidity, 3. ...")

**Demo moment:** "Here are 12 features Attio's PM team should build next quarter, ranked by how often AI engines surface the gap when describing HubSpot."

**Why it wins:** Reframes Peec from a marketing tool into a product tool. Judges hear something new. Distribution wedge: small brands can ship features faster than giants — give them a target list.

**Why it could fail:** Doesn't directly answer "win distribution" — it's a meta-answer ("ship the right product, distribution follows"). Some judges will read it as off-spec.

---

## Perspective 2: "Founders are the moat"

**The shift:** Big brands are faceless. Underdogs have founders (Carl Pei, Nicolas Bustamante, Wang Chuanfu) who can punch above their weight on social. AI engines increasingly cite tweets, podcasts, founder LinkedIn posts. Founder voice is asymmetric — Tim Cook can't tweet about transparency vs. plastic phones the way Carl Pei can.

**What you build:** **"Founder Signal"** — uses Peec's topic + sentiment data to identify the 3 conversations this week where the founder's voice would move the needle most. Generates: tweet thread, podcast pitch (with which podcasts based on cited domains), LinkedIn post — all in the founder's actual voice (you train on their past posts).

**Demo moment:** "Carl Pei should tweet about [X] this week because Peec shows ChatGPT is increasingly citing podcast appearances on this topic, and Nothing is invisible there. Here's the tweet, the 3 podcasts to pitch, and the LinkedIn variant."

**Why it wins:** Concrete and demoable without latency issues — you're producing artifacts, judges read them. Voice-cloning a real founder is a memorable demo. The output isn't generic content, it's a personalized weekly briefing for ONE specific person (the founder).

**Why it could fail:** Voice-cloning a real founder without permission is sketchy. You could get hammered in Q&A. Mitigation: frame it as a *brief for the founder's team*, not auto-posted. But weaker as a demo.

---

## Perspective 3: "Find the empty rooms"

**The shift:** Everyone fights over crowded prompts ("best CRM"). The real underdog play is **topic land-grabbing** — find prompts/topics where NO brand dominates yet, plant a comprehensive flag, own the category before anyone notices.

**What you build:** **"Topic Vacuum Finder"** — scans `get_brand_report` for prompts where total share-of-voice is low, no clear winner exists, and the underdog could realistically own them with one cornerstone piece. Then generates the cornerstone resource (long-form guide / open-source tool / public dataset) designed to be the canonical answer.

**Demo moment:** "We found 7 unowned topics in the CRM space. For 'best CRM for AI-native startups,' nobody dominates — Attio could own this with this single resource. Here it is."

**Why it wins:** Strategic, not tactical. The output is a defensible asset, not a Reddit post. Small brands LOVE this kind of insight because they don't have the headcount to fight everywhere — they need to know where to fight.

**Why it could fail:** Hard to *prove it works* in 48 hours since topic dominance takes weeks. Best demo is the strategic recommendation + a draft of the cornerstone piece. Less "live data flowing" theater.

---

## Perspective 4: "The category's misinformation problem is your wedge"

**The shift:** AI engines confidently make stuff up. They cite outdated pricing, wrong feature lists, deprecated APIs, even hallucinated facts. For underdogs this is brutal — ChatGPT might be telling people you don't have a feature you shipped 6 months ago. But it's also an opening: prove you're the most accurate, current, structured source and AI will increasingly trust you.

**What you build:** **"Truth Audit"** — `list_chats` filtered to your brand → `get_chat` to extract every factual claim AI made about you → Claude classifies each as True / Outdated / Wrong → for the wrong/outdated ones, identifies which cited URL caused the error → drafts a correction outreach OR a structured fact-page on your own domain that AI will cite over the wrong source.

**Demo moment:** "ChatGPT told 31 users this week that Attio costs $X. It actually costs $Y. The wrong number traces to this Capterra page from 2024. Here's the outreach email, here's a structured pricing page that will outrank it within Perplexity's index in days."

**Why it wins:** Demonstrably useful from minute one. Defensive, but defense IS distribution at this stage — every wrong AI mention is a lost lead. Easy to verify the demo is real (judges can re-query and see the wrong info themselves). Underdogs care intensely about this — giants have PR teams, you don't.

**Why it could fail:** Less "agentic," more "audit tool." Judges who reward novelty over usefulness might not bite. Also, you can't prove the correction *worked* in 48 hours.

---

## Brutal comparison

| Idea | Demo-day risk | Novelty | Fits "distribution" frame | Defensibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voice of the Market (research) | Low | **High** | Stretch | Strong |
| Founder Signal (founder voice) | Medium (ethics) | High | Direct | Medium |
| Topic Vacuum Finder | Low | Medium | Direct | Strong |
| Truth Audit | **Lowest** | Medium | Indirect | **Strongest** |

## My honest call

**Truth Audit** is the safest bet that still has a strong story. **Voice of the Market** is the riskiest but the only one that will make a judge say "I haven't seen that before." If you want to win the track outright, Voice of the Market. If you want to ship cleanly and place top-3, Truth Audit.

Topic Vacuum Finder is the third option I'd take seriously — it's strategic and the output is a defensible asset, but the demo will feel slower than the other two.

Founder Signal is the splashiest demo but the ethics question will eat 5 minutes of your Q&A.

Tell me which angle resonates and I'll pressure-test it harder before we touch code. Or if none of these land, I can keep going — there are at least 6 more angles I didn't write up.

lastPrompt: look at it from different prespectives, come up with new idea for the hackathon

content.text: I really liked the founder thing, that help them to create UGC videos that goes viral and also cited by these LLMs. search through the tools and even MCP of peec AI, lets create a plan

Good direction. Let me stress-test it before we plan, because "viral + cited by LLMs" has two contradictions baked in.

## Brutal pre-plan critique

**1. "Going viral" is not engineerable.** Nobody can guarantee it — not MrBeast's team, not agencies. If you pitch "viral video generator" the judges will eat you alive. Reframe to: *"founder content slate that maximizes the conditions for reach AND citation."* Probability uplift, not promise.

**2. Viral and cited are two different channels.** I checked Peec's docs and the data confirms this:
- **TikTok / Reels / Shorts**: drive virality. Almost never cited by LLMs.
- **YouTube long-form**: cited heavily by Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, ChatGPT browse — citation comes from the **transcript + title + description + chapters**, not the video itself.
- **Podcast appearances**: cited via transcripts and show notes.

So a single video can't do both. You need a **two-track slate** that compounds: Shorts pull attention → searches → long-form watch → transcripts indexed → AI cites you.

**3. Peec helps with citation, not virality.** Be honest: Peec gives you topic intelligence, sub-query intelligence (the gold), cited-channel intelligence, and language intelligence. It does NOT tell you what's trending on TikTok. For the virality side you'll either ignore the data layer (Claude generates hot-take scripts from Peec topics) or pull a separate trends source. Don't pretend Peec does this.

**4. You can't prove citation OR virality in 48h.** Citations take 1-4 weeks to land in indexes. Virality is a coin flip. Your demo has to prove the **targeting precision** and the **loop**, not the outcomes. Replay against a real founder video that already got cited.

---

## The actual plan: "Founder Channel Engine"

A weekly content slate generator for one underdog founder (Carl Pei / Nicolas Bustamante / Wang Chuanfu), targeting the asymmetric advantage that giants don't have a relatable face.

### What it produces (the slate)

For one founder, one week, one project:

**Track A — Citation play (1 long-form YouTube video)**
- Topic: derived from `peec_topic_heatmap` → blind spot with high opportunity
- Hook + chapters: derived from `list_search_queries` → these are the literal sub-queries AI engines fan out to. Each becomes a video chapter title. This means the transcript directly answers what LLMs are searching for.
- Language patterns: derived from `get_url_content` on top-cited videos in the topic → mimic the structural patterns AI prefers
- Title + description + chapter timestamps + tags → all citation-optimized
- Output: full brief, script outline, metadata package

**Track B — Reach play (3 short-form video scripts)**
- Topics: `get_brand_report` filtered to negative competitor sentiment → these are hot-take wedges (where AI says something unflattering about Apple/Salesforce/Tesla, the founder reacts)
- Script: hook (3 sec), claim (15 sec), evidence (20 sec), CTA (5 sec)
- B-roll plan: phone-only shoot list
- On-screen text + hashtags
- Posting time recommendation

**Track C — Distribution graph (5 channel partnership pitches)**
- `get_url_report` filtered to YouTube `classification` for the topic → top channels currently being cited
- For each: channel name, why they matter (cited X times for prompt Y), pitch angle, suggested email
- These become the founder's guest-podcast / collab pipeline

**Track D — Predicted impact**
- Current Peec baseline: visibility, share-of-voice, cited URL count
- Per-asset projection: "If this YouTube video lands, here are the 7 sub-queries it could win"
- Honest framing: "We can't promise virality, but here's the targeting precision."

### The Peec MCP wiring (concrete)

| Slate component | Peec tools used |
|---|---|
| Topic selection (Track A) | `peec_topic_heatmap` slash prompt → blind spots |
| Sub-query → chapters | `list_search_queries` filtered to top topic |
| Language patterns | `get_url_report` (top YouTube cited) → `get_url_content` |
| Hot-take wedges (Track B) | `get_brand_report` with sentiment dimension on competitors |
| Channel pitch list (Track C) | `get_url_report` with `classification=youtube` (or equivalent) |
| Baseline + projection (Track D) | `get_brand_report` for current state + `list_prompts` for target prompts |
| Replay validation (demo) | `list_chats` + `get_chat` on prompts where founder's existing videos are cited |

### The demo (90 seconds, choreographed)

**Frame 1 (15s):** "Carl Pei has 5 hours a week to make content. We tell him exactly what to film." Open the tool, type `founder=carl_pei project=nothing week=this`.

**Frame 2 (30s):** Live MCP calls fire on screen. Topic heatmap → blind spot found ("Camera quality narratives, where Apple wins 73%"). Fanout queries appear. Top-cited YouTube competitor video appears. Tool extracts language patterns.

**Frame 3 (30s):** Slate renders:
- 1 YouTube brief: *"The Real Reason Phone Cameras Look the Same Now (Nothing Phone (3) Camera Walkthrough)"* — chapter list maps 1:1 to the 6 fanout sub-queries from Peec
- 3 Shorts scripts: hot takes on Apple's "computational photography" narrative, where Peec shows soft sentiment
- 5 channel pitches: MKBHD, Mrwhosetheboss, etc. with custom pitch angles based on Peec citation data

**Frame 4 (15s) — the killer slide:** "How do we know this works? Here's a video Carl Pei published 3 months ago that earned 4.2M views and 31 AI citations. Here's the brief our tool would have generated for it. Same hook, same chapters, same channel pitches. We're not promising virality — we're showing that the Peec-targeted brief structure matches what already wins."

### The proof problem, solved

You cannot prove "viral + cited" end-to-end in 48 hours. Three things you CAN show:
1. **Targeting precision**: chapter titles literally match Peec's fanout sub-queries — verifiable on stage
2. **Pattern matching**: existing viral+cited videos retroactively fit the brief our tool generates — replay
3. **Optional flex**: actually film and post ONE 30-second Short during the hackathon (you on camera saying the script). It won't go viral but it proves the loop closes — script → publish → exists on TikTok.

---

## Architecture

Recommend: **CLI + simple web preview** (Next.js with one route).
- CLI calls Peec MCP server-side, runs the pipeline, generates a JSON slate
- Web UI (one page) renders the slate beautifully — judges scroll through artifacts
- Option to "regenerate Track B" for live demo theater

Stack:
- TypeScript (Bun or Node)
- Anthropic SDK (Claude for generation, with prompt caching on the founder voice samples)
- Peec MCP via the SDK's MCP client
- Next.js 15 + Tailwind for the preview UI
- Optional: ElevenLabs for voice preview of the script

### Build phases for 48h

**Phase 1 (4h) — Plumbing.** Peec MCP connected, can pull `list_projects`, `get_brand_report`, `list_search_queries` for the chosen brand. Test queries in REPL.

**Phase 2 (6h) — Track A pipeline.** Topic selection → sub-query extraction → competitor video scrape → Claude generates the long-form brief. Iterate until quality bar is met.

**Phase 3 (4h) — Track B pipeline.** Sentiment-driven topic selection → 3 Shorts scripts. Voice-tune to founder.

**Phase 4 (3h) — Track C pipeline.** Channel pitch list with custom emails.

**Phase 5 (3h) — Web UI.** Single page renders the full slate with sections. Tailwind + minimal polish.

**Phase 6 (4h) — Replay validation.** Pick 3 real founder videos that already got cited (you'll need to find these in Peec data). Generate the brief our tool would have made. Side-by-side comparison slide for the demo.

**Phase 7 (2h) — Demo prep.** 90-sec script, choreography, fallback plan if Peec MCP times out on stage (cache responses).

**Buffer (8h)** for the inevitable problems: Peec auth, rate limits, MCP weirdness, voice-cloning ethics rewrite if needed, sleep.

---

## Three things to decide before I write code

1. **Which founder/brand?** Carl Pei (Nothing) is the strongest pick — most existing video footage to train voice on, most active social presence, most replay material. Nicolas Bustamante (Attio) is sharp but sparse. Wang Chuanfu (BYD) is almost invisible in Western media. **Strong rec: Carl Pei.**

2. **Voice-cloning the founder — yes or no?** Generating scripts "in their voice" is fine. Actually cloning audio with ElevenLabs is sketchy and might cost you in Q&A. **Rec: brief + script only, no audio clone, no avatar.** Frame as "for the founder's content team to film with the founder."

3. **Do we publish a real Short during the hackathon?** Risky (your face on a brand demo) but powerful (closes the loop visibly). **Rec: yes, but post from a personal/test account, not impersonating the brand. Make it a 20-sec "this is what our tool generated" meta-video.**

If you confirm those three (or override), I'll start scaffolding the repo. Want me to also fetch the Peec API auth flow specifics so we don't lose 2 hours on OAuth setup tomorrow?
we want to have our own face and brand probably, no voice clone or anything and yes publish soth

Locked. That actually unlocks a cleaner demo because we sidestep the impersonation/ethics minefield entirely. Three quick reframes from your call:

**1. The published Short becomes a tool artifact, not impersonation.**
The cleanest play: pick one Shorts script the tool generates for, say, Nothing — a fan-perspective hot take ("Why I think Nothing's transparent design is the only honest move in phones") — and one of you delivers it to camera. You're presenting AS enthusiasts/observers, which is literally what UGC is. The tool produced the script, you produced the human, both are real. No fake founder.

**2. Your team's brand becomes the wrapper, not the subject.**
The Short is published from your team's account/handle (the hackathon project brand). The CONTENT is about Nothing. Caption: "Made with [your tool name] — script generated from real AI search data." This makes the demo recursive: your tool's first published output is also the marketing for your tool.

**3. You also get a built-in second demo asset.**
The Short doubles as a deliverable judges can pull up on their phones. Way more memorable than slides.

## Three remaining decisions before I scaffold

**Decision 1 — Data brand (the Peec project we pull from)**
Strongly recommend **Nothing**. Why: most consumer UGC happens here, fastest to film, most fan-energy to channel, judges instantly grok the David vs Goliath. Attio is dev-niche and harder to film authentically. BYD is geo-fragmented and you don't have the regional context.

**Decision 2 — Team/product brand for the wrapper**
Do you have a name for the tool yet? Need it for: the GitHub repo, the Next.js page header, the published Short caption, the demo title slide. If not, I can suggest a few — what's your team's vibe (technical/serious vs playful/punchy)?

**Decision 3 — Stack**
Recommend: **TypeScript + Bun + Next.js 15 + Tailwind + Anthropic SDK with MCP client**.
- Bun for speed (less waiting on installs at 2am)
- Next.js for the demo UI (one page is enough)
- Anthropic SDK has MCP client baked in → connects to Peec without us writing transport code
- Skip a database — JSON files are fine for 48h

Override if you'd rather Python (Streamlit demo UI is faster but uglier) or pure CLI.

## What I'll scaffold once you confirm

```
founder-channel-engine/
├── packages/
│   ├── core/              # Pipeline: Peec calls + Claude generation
│   │   ├── peec-client.ts # MCP wrapper, typed
│   │   ├── tracks/
│   │   │   ├── long-form.ts   # Track A: YouTube brief
│   │   │   ├── shorts.ts      # Track B: 3 Shorts scripts
│   │   │   ├── pitches.ts     # Track C: channel pitch list
│   │   │   └── projection.ts  # Track D: baseline + targeting
│   │   └── slate.ts       # Orchestrator
│   └── web/               # Next.js demo UI
│       └── app/page.tsx   # Renders the slate
├── scripts/
│   └── generate-slate.ts  # CLI entry: `bun run slate --brand=nothing`
└── .env.example           # PEEC_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
```

Confirm the three decisions (or override any of them) and I'll start with the Peec MCP client and a working `list_projects` call so we know the auth path is clean before anything else. That's the highest-risk piece — if the OAuth flow fights us, we need to know in hour 1, not hour 30.

input.prompt: Give me the exact, complete steps to connect the Peec AI MCP server to Claude Code (the CLI/IDE tool from Anthropic). Include: the exact server URL, transport type, the OAuth flow, any CLI commands like `claude mcp add`, any JSON config snippets for settings files, and any prerequisites. Be exhaustive — include every command and config example shown on the page, verbatim if possible.

input.command: claude mcp list 2>&1 | head -50
input.description: List currently configured MCP servers

content.content:
# Connecting Peec AI MCP to Claude Code (CLI)

## Single Command Setup

Execute this terminal command:

```bash
claude mcp add peec-ai --transport streamable-http https://api.peec.ai/mcp
```

## Server Details

- **URL:** `https://api.peec.ai/mcp`
- **Transport Type:** Streamable HTTP

## Authorization

The system will prompt you to sign in via Peec AI the first time you use any Peec AI tool within Claude Code.

## Verification

After setup completes, test the connection by asking Claude Code to list your projects. You should receive a response showing projects accessible to your account.

## Troubleshooting

If authorization fails, verify you're using the correct Peec AI credentials, remove and re-add the integration, or clear browser cookies for `api.peec.ai`. For persistent issues, contact support@peec.ai.

content.text:
Registered and waiting on auth. Now you need to do the OAuth flow.

## Action required from you

In Claude Code, run this slash command:

```
/mcp
```

That opens the MCP server panel. Find **peec-ai**, hit **Authenticate** — your browser will open, you sign in with your Peec account, approve the consent screen, and the tab will close. After that the status flips to `✓ Connected` and ~27 tools (15 read-only + 12 write) become callable in this session.

Note: I had to use `--transport http` instead of `streamable-http` (the docs are slightly out of date — the CLI flag is just `http`, which uses the streamable-HTTP transport under the hood). Server registered fine.

Once you've authenticated, tell me and I'll immediately:
1. Call `list_projects` to confirm we see the Nothing/Attio/BYD test projects
2. Pull `list_brands`, `list_topics`, `list_models` for Nothing so we know exactly what data we're working with
3. Test one heavy call (`get_brand_report` for last 30 days) so we know the response shapes before we write any pipeline code

This is the highest-risk hour — if Peec's data for the test project is sparse or shaped weird, we adapt the plan before we waste build time. Ping me when the OAuth tab closes.

this is my apikey, you do the rest
REDACTED

One quick safety note: that key is now in this chat transcript — rotate it after the hackathon (Peec dashboard → API keys → revoke). Until then I'll keep it in a local `.env`, never committed.

Now let me check whether the MCP server accepts API-key auth (vs only OAuth) and grab the REST auth docs as a fallback path.

content.content:
# Peec API Authentication Summary

## Header Authentication
**Header name:** `x-api-key`
**Prefix:** None (direct key value)

Example:
```bash
curl -X GET "https://api.peec.ai/customer/v1/prompts" \
  -H "x-api-key: REDACTED"
```

## Alternative: Query Parameter
The API also accepts authentication via query string:
```bash
curl -X GET "https://api.peec.ai/customer/v1/prompts?api_key=REDACTED"
```

## Base URL
`https://api.peec.ai/customer/v1/`

## MCP Server Authentication
The documentation does not specify whether API keys from the standard Customer API can authenticate against the MCP server at `https://api.peec.ai/mcp`, nor does it detail OAuth integration requirements for that endpoint.

## Security Note
The documentation recommends using "the x-api-key header for better security" rather than query parameters, and emphasizes keeping keys secret while using project-scoped keys where feasible.

content.text:
Two findings — important:

1. **MCP rejects API keys** (both `Authorization: Bearer` and `x-api-key`). MCP server is OAuth-only.
2. **REST API works**. We have access to the Nothing test project: `or_faaa7625-bc84-4f64-a754-a412d423c641` (and Attio: `or_47ccb54e-0f32-4c95-b460-6a070499d084`).

Strategic call: I'll use the REST API now to explore the data shape and start building, **and you should authenticate the MCP in Claude Code via `/mcp` in parallel** — the challenge says "use the Peec MCP," so we'll wire MCP into the demo flow once OAuth is done. The REST work isn't wasted; it's the same endpoints.

Let me lock the key safely and explore Nothing's data shape.

content.text:
Real data is rich. Quick read:

- **10 brands** tracked (Nothing is `is_own:true`, competitors: Apple, Google, Samsung, Xiaomi, OnePlus, Sony, Pixel, Bose, Logitech)
- **5 topics**: Consumer Tech Innovation, Wireless Audio, Mobile Ecosystem, Smartphone Design, Minimalist Hardware
- **51 prompts** with real fan-bait questions ("Why is Nothing considered a disruptor", "What are the most aesthetic phones with unique transparent hardware")
- **3 active models**: ChatGPT, Gemini, Google AI Overview — that's our citation surface

Let me save these IDs and validate the heavier endpoints (brand report, chats, URL report) in parallel before I start writing pipeline code.

todos.content: Connect to Peec (REST + MCP), validate auth
todos.activeForm: Connecting to Peec and validating auth

todos.content: Explore Nothing project data shape (brands, topics, prompts, models, brand report, chats, URL report)
Scaffold TypeScript project (Bun + Next.js + Anthropic SDK + Peec REST client)
Build Peec REST client with typed wrappers for our pipeline endpoints
Build Track A pipeline: long-form YouTube brief from topic + fanout queries
Build Track B pipeline: 3 Shorts scripts from sentiment-driven competitor wedges
Build Track C pipeline: channel pitch list from cited YouTube domains
Build Next.js demo UI rendering the slate
Generate replay validation: existing viral founder video → brief our tool would have made
Film and publish one Short during the hackathon

content.text:
Massive find. Real data, real story:

**Nothing has the BEST avg position (2.39) — better than Apple (3.56).** Their problem isn't *how* they're mentioned, it's *how often*: 19.9% visibility vs Apple's 50%. That reframes the whole pitch — **"Nothing wins when it shows up. The job is to make it show up more."** This is exactly the kind of insight our tool needs to surface in the demo.

# Peec.ai Docs

## Docs

- [Peec AI for Agencies: Getting started](https://docs.peec.ai/agencies/agency-getting-started.md)
- [Managing your projects](https://docs.peec.ai/agencies/managing-your-projects.md)
- [Understanding credits](https://docs.peec.ai/agencies/understanding_credits.md): How agencies can use credits to allocate prompts and models to their projects
- [List Projects](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/company/list-projects.md): List the projects of a company
- [Accept Brand Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/accept-brand-suggestion.md): Accept a brand suggestion by ID, converting it into a brand within the project
- [Accept Prompt Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/accept-prompt-suggestion.md): Accept a prompt suggestion by ID, creating a new prompt from it
- [Accept Topic Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/accept-topic-suggestion.md): Accept a topic suggestion by ID, converting it into a regular topic
- [Create Brand](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/create-brand.md): Create a new brand within a project
- [Create Prompt](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/create-prompt.md): Create a new prompt within a project
- [Create Tag](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/create-tag.md): Create a new tag within a project
- [Create Topic](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/create-topic.md): Create a new topic within a project
- [Delete Brand](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/delete-brand.md): Soft-delete a brand within a project.
- [Delete Prompt](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/delete-prompt.md): Soft-delete a prompt and cascade to related chats
- [Delete Tag](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/delete-tag.md): Soft-delete a tag within a project
- [Delete Topic](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/delete-topic.md): Soft-delete a topic, detaching all associated prompts and deleting prompt suggestions
- [Get Chat](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/get-chat.md): Get a single chat
- [List Brand Suggestions](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-brand-suggestions.md): List the open brand suggestions of a project
- [List Brands](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-brands.md): List the brands of a project
- [List Chats](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-chats.md): List the chats of a project
- [List Fanout Search Queries](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-fanout-search-queries.md): List the fanout search queries of a project
- [List Fanout Shopping Queries](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-fanout-shopping-queries.md): List the fanout shopping queries of a project
- [List Model Channels](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-model-channels.md): List the model channels
- [List Models](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-models.md): Deprecated: use List Model Channels instead.
- [List Prompt Suggestions](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-prompt-suggestions.md): List the prompt suggestions of a project
- [List Prompts](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-prompts.md): List the prompts of a project
- [List Tags](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-tags.md): List the tags of a project
- [List Topic Suggestions](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-topic-suggestions.md): List the topic suggestions of a project
- [List Topics](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/list-topics.md): List the topics of a project
- [Reject Brand Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/reject-brand-suggestion.md): Reject a brand suggestion by ID, removing it from the project and preventing it from being re-suggested
- [Reject Prompt Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/reject-prompt-suggestion.md): Reject a prompt suggestion by ID, soft-deleting it
- [Reject Topic Suggestion](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/reject-topic-suggestion.md): Reject a topic suggestion by ID, soft-deleting it and its associated prompt suggestions
- [Update Brand](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/update-brand.md): Update a brand within a project. Changes to name, regex, or aliases trigger a background recalculation of metrics. While recalculation is in progress, further updates to these fields are blocked (409 Conflict) until it completes.
- [Update Prompt](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/update-prompt.md): Update a prompt's topic and tags within a project
- [Update Tag](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/update-tag.md): Update a tag within a project
- [Update Topic](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/project/update-topic.md): Update a topic within a project
- [Get Brands Report](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/reports/get-brands-report.md): Get a report on Brands.
- [Get Domains Report](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/reports/get-domains-report.md): Get a report on Source Domains
- [Get URL Content](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/reports/get-url-content.md): Return the scraped markdown content of a source URL. Use the URLs report to discover URLs.
- [Get URLs Report](https://docs.peec.ai/api-reference/reports/get-urls-report.md): Get a report on Source URLs
- [Authentication for Peec API](https://docs.peec.ai/api/authentication.md)
- [Changelog](https://docs.peec.ai/api/changelog.md): Track updates, improvements, and breaking changes to the Peec API.
- [Introduction to Peec API](https://docs.peec.ai/api/introduction.md)
- [Model Channels](https://docs.peec.ai/api/model-channels.md): A stable way to reference AI surfaces as underlying models evolve.
- [Rate Limits for Peec API](https://docs.peec.ai/api/ratelimits.md)
- [Crawl Insights](https://docs.peec.ai/crawl-insights.md)
- [Crawlability](https://docs.peec.ai/crawlability.md)
- [Domains](https://docs.peec.ai/domains.md)
- [Get inspired by Actions](https://docs.peec.ai/get-inspired-by-actions.md): Learn how Actions translate source-level data into clear opportunities to improve your AI visibility.
- [Identifying your competitors](https://docs.peec.ai/identifying-your-competitors.md): Track competitive performance and see who AI is promoting alongside (or instead of) you.
- [Welcome to Peec AI](https://docs.peec.ai/intro-to-peec-ai.md): The #1 AI search analytics tool for marketing teams and agencies.
- [Looker Studio connector](https://docs.peec.ai/looker/introduction.md): Complete guide to connecting and using Peec AI data in Looker Studio.
- [Manage your project](https://docs.peec.ai/manage-your-project.md)
- [MCP Server](https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/introduction.md): Connect AI assistants like Claude, Cursor, and other MCP-compatible tools directly to your Peec AI data. Ask questions in plain language and get answers from the same data the dashboard shows.
- [Prompts](https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/prompts.md): Peec AI ships native MCP prompts: ready-to-run analysis workflows exposed as slash commands in your AI tool. Each prompt runs a scripted sequence of tool calls and formats the output for you.
- [Setup Guide](https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/setup.md): Step-by-step instructions for connecting the Peec AI MCP Server to Claude, Cursor, and other AI tools. Stuck? Email support@peec.ai.
- [Tools Reference](https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/tools.md): Every tool the Peec AI MCP Server exposes, with parameters and response fields.
- [Use Cases](https://docs.peec.ai/mcp/use-cases.md): Example prompts and workflows for the Peec AI MCP Server. Browse by type if you need ideas on what to ask.
- [Metrics Overview](https://docs.peec.ai/metrics-overview.md): Understanding all the key metrics that drive your AI search optimization strategy.
- [Position](https://docs.peec.ai/metrics/brand-metrics/position.md): How your brand ranks in AI responses.
- [Sentiment](https://docs.peec.ai/metrics/brand-metrics/sentiment.md): The tone and sentiment of your brand mentions.
- [Share of Voice](https://docs.peec.ai/metrics/brand-metrics/share-of-voice.md)
- [Visibility](https://docs.peec.ai/metrics/brand-metrics/visibility.md): How often your brand is mentioned in AI responses.
- [Organizing your setup](https://docs.peec.ai/organizing-your-setup.md): Keep your prompts manageable and analysis-ready with smart organization.
- [Brand profile](https://docs.peec.ai/project-profile.md)
- [Quickstart Guide](https://docs.peec.ai/quickstart-guide.md): The fastest way to understand your brand’s AI search presence.
- [Setting up your prompts](https://docs.peec.ai/setting-up-your-prompts.md): Our in-depth guide to building your prompt and competitor tracking system.
- [Sidebar Navigation ](https://docs.peec.ai/sidebar-navigation.md): Quick reference to all navigation sections and their key features.
- [Understanding chats](https://docs.peec.ai/understanding-chats.md): Learn how to read the AI responses that form the foundation of all your analytics.
- [Understanding sources](https://docs.peec.ai/understanding-sources.md): Discover which websites AI models trust and learn how to optimize your presence across them.
- [Understanding your performance](https://docs.peec.ai/understanding-your-performance.md): Your main analytics hub for tracking AI search performance across prompts and competitors.
- [URLs](https://docs.peec.ai/urls.md)
- [Use Cases](https://docs.peec.ai/use-cases.md)
- [Video Library](https://docs.peec.ai/video-library.md): For those who prefer the video format, this is a collection of all of the videos we've linked in this Product Documentation.

## OpenAPI Specs
